notes: MB embedded release (Blue Note BST 84078, 1961, Jimmy Smith) and stored wiki page both match our album. Prose mirrors the wiki lead: recorded 1960, released on Blue Note, same session as Back at the Chicken Shack (1960) - all confirmed. All 5 keyTracks on the MB/wiki tracklist.

MB embedded (Atlantic SD 1361, 1961) and wiki page match. Prose claims all confirmed by the wiki extract: released March 1961 on Atlantic, first album with Coltrane on soprano saxophone, edited title track a 1961 radio hit, major commercial success. Ours says 'first released album' where wiki says 'first album' - consistent. All 4 keyTracks match the MB tracklist.

MB embedded (Candid CJM 8027, 1961-10) and wiki page match. Prose first sentence is the wiki lead verbatim; 'We Speak' and 'Strength and Sanity' confirmed on the tracklist; 'hard bop context' / 'interpretive range' are editorial. All 5 keyTracks on the 7-track album.

MB embedded confirms Max Roach, 1961, impulse! AS-8, and all 5 keyTracks. No wikipedia evidence (no stored URL; opensearch returned nothing). The Booker Little claims - that this was his penultimate recording and that he died of uremia in early October 1961 - cannot be checked against pack evidence (no personnel data anywhere in the item). Right entity, no contradictions.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Captures clarinetist Phil Woods during the hard bop period
wrongClaims.problem: Wrong instrument for Woods on this album: the wiki lead calls him a saxophonist and the personnel list credits him on alto saxophone only; our own first sentence also says 'saxophonist Phil Woods'
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Rights of Swing is an album led by saxophonist Phil Woods ... Personnel: Phil Woods - alto saxophone

notes: Everything else checks out: MB embedded (Candid CJS 9016, 1961) and wiki match, and all 5 keyTracks match exactly. 'Clarinetist' looks like a template fill-in error. Woods may have played clarinet elsewhere in his career (background knowledge, not pack evidence), but the evidence for this album shows alto saxophone only.

fieldIssues.evidence: Oliver Nelson (leader); Dolphy is a featured sideman
fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Screamin' the Blues is an album by American saxophonist Oliver Nelson, originally released in 1961 on New Jazz Records.
originally released in 1961 on New Jazz Records

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Screamin' the Blues is an album by Eric Dolphy, released in 1961 on Prestige
wrongClaims.problem: Leader is Oliver Nelson, not Dolphy (MB bills 'Oliver Nelson Sextet' first, with Dolphy and Richard Williams as featured artists; wiki personnel has Nelson on tenor/alto, Dolphy as sideman on bass clarinet/alto); original label was New Jazz, not Prestige
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Screamin' the Blues is an album by American saxophonist Oliver Nelson, originally released in 1961 on New Jazz Records.

notes: Same album, wrong leader: prose and the artist field attribute the album to Eric Dolphy, but both sources show it as an Oliver Nelson date with Dolphy as sideman. MB embedded doc is the 1991 OJC reissue (OJCCD-080-2) crediting Oliver Nelson Sextet / Eric Dolphy / Richard Williams; release-group first release 1961-01, so year 1961 is fine. All 5 keyTracks match the tracklist. On label: New Jazz was a Prestige-family imprint (the Far Cry wiki lead in this same pack calls New Jazz 'a subsidiary of the Prestige label'), but the dataset elsewhere uses 'New Jazz' as its own label value (the Where? item), so this field should read New Jazz. Stored wiki link does point to the correct album page.

fieldIssues.evidence: not on the album; both tracklists show six Silver tunes without it

notes: MB (search, artist similarity 1.0; Prestige 7240, 1961) and wiki agree on a six-track album: Señor Blues, Moon Ray/Moonray, Sister Sadie, Doodlin', The Preacher, Strollin'. 'Nica's Dream' is not on it (it is a well-known Horace Silver composition - background knowledge, which may explain the slip - but it is absent from both tracklists). The other 3 keyTracks match. Year 1961 matches MB; wiki shortDescription says '1962 studio album' while its extract says recorded 1961 - sources differ on release year, ours sides with MB. Also note the item id 'shirley-scott-trio' vs title 'Shirley Scott Plays Horace Silver'.

MB embedded (Prestige PRLP 7195, 1961) and wiki match. Prose mirrors the wiki lead: recorded mainly 1958 for Prestige, not released until 1961 as PRLP 7195 - catalog number confirmed by MB too. All 5 keyTracks on the 8-track album. The era-template sentence ('reconnecting jazz with blues and gospel...') is editorial.

MB (search, artist similarity 1.0; Candid CJM 8015, 1961) and wiki agree; prose lead matches the wiki verbatim. KeyTrack 'African Woman' should be 'African Lady' per both the MB and wiki tracklists; the other three keyTracks match. 'Part of the cultural moment that included We Insist!' is a contextual claim not checkable against pack evidence (We Insist! appears nowhere in the evidence), and the closing sentence is editorial.

MB (search, artist similarity 1.0; Riverside RLP 376, 1961, Live release-group) and wiki match. Prose opening is the wiki lead verbatim: trio with bassist Scott LaFaro and drummer Paul Motian, released 1961, routinely ranked among the best live jazz recordings. All 4 keyTracks match. The 'redefined the piano trio format' material is editorial.

fieldIssues.ours: Whistle While You Work; He's a Real Gone Guy
fieldIssues.evidence: not on the album; the wiki page lists a complete six-track program
fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: "Whap!" – 4:26 / "I Want a Little Girl" (Murray Mencher, Billy Moll) – 6:47 / "The Honeydripper" (Joe Liggins) – 8:14 / "Dink's Blues" – 7:59 / "Mr. Lucky" (Henry Mancini) – 5:03 / "Blues and Tonic" – 5:02

notes: Stored wiki page is about our album and confirms 1961, Prestige, and Grant Green on guitar (also Jimmy Forrest on tenor). MB found only a release-group candidate ('Brother' Jack McDuff, 1961) with no release/tracklist - weak per spec - so the tracklist check rests on the wiki's complete six-track listing, which lacks 'Whistle While You Work' and 'He's a Real Gone Guy'. Those two likely belong to another McDuff record, not determinable from pack evidence. The Jimmy Smith comparison, 'signature album', and soul-jazz/jazz-funk influence claims are editorial, not flagged.

MB embedded (Jazzland JLP 946S, 1961) and wiki match. Prose is the wiki lead verbatim: 1961 album on Jazzland (a Riverside subsidiary), material recorded four years earlier when Monk worked extensively with Coltrane - the 1957 session dates in the extract bear out 'four years earlier'. All 5 keyTracks match the MB tracklist.

fieldIssues.ours: Reza; Sweet Little Maia; Keiko's Birthday March; Village Greene; Jay-Ree; For Heaven's Sake

notes: Prose lead is the wiki text verbatim and checks out (co-led by Philly Joe Jones and Elvin Jones, Atlantic, recorded 1961, with Mitchell/Fuller/Mobley/Kelly/Chambers). But the ENTIRE keyTracks list is wrong: the authoritative embedded-MBID tracklist (Le Roi, Beau-Ty, Brown Sugar) and the identical wiki tracklist show a three-track album; none of our six titles is on it - they look imported from a different Elvin Jones album, not identifiable from pack evidence. The trailing sentence 'His brothers Hank and Thad were also celebrated jazz musicians...' is a dangling artist-bio fragment about Elvin Jones, unverifiable here. Release year: ours 1961 = MB embedded 1961; wiki shortDescription says '1964 studio album' - sources conflict, noted for the repair pass.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate: "1962" (Riverside RLP-9399); disambiguation entry: "Waltz for Debby (1962 album), album by Bill Evans"

wrongClaims.ourClaim: Waltz for Debby is the name of:"Waltz for Debby" (song), a 1956 jazz composition by Bill Evans later made into a song
wrongClaims.problem: The albumDNA is scraped Wikipedia disambiguation-page text, not prose about the album - it describes the term and three different entities (a 1956 song, our album, and the 1964 Monica Zetterlund/Bill Evans album)
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Waltz for Debby is the name of: "Waltz for Debby" (song), a 1956 jazz composition by Bill Evans later made into a song / Waltz for Debby (1962 album), album by Bill Evans / Waltz for Debby (1964 album), album by Monica Zetterlund and Bill Evans

notes: Classic disambiguation-collision case (the known disease). The stored URL resolves to the 'Waltz for Debby' disambiguation page, and the prose is that page's text plus two appended editorial sentences; it never describes the album itself. The catalog entry is salvageable: the album is MB-verified (Bill Evans Trio, Riverside RLP-9399, Live, first release 1962) and all 4 keyTracks match the MB tracklist. Repairs: rewrite the prose about the 1962 live album, set year to 1962 (ours says 1961; MB and the disambig entry both say 1962 - 1961 is the recording year per background knowledge, not citable), and repoint the wiki link to the album article ('Waltz for Debby (1962 album)' per the disambiguation listing).

Prose is the wiki lead verbatim: Ron Carter's debut, recorded 1961 at Van Gelder Studio, New Jazz label, 'Some reissues of the album appear under Eric Dolphy's name.' MB embedded (New Jazz NJ/NJLP 8265, 1961) co-credits Ron Carter / Eric Dolphy / Mal Waldron, and all 5 keyTracks match. The artist field says only 'Eric Dolphy' even though the album is canonically Carter's - defensible given the wiki's reissue-crediting note and the MB co-credit, so not flagged as a contradiction, but worth an editorial look: our own prose calls it Carter's debut while the site files it under Dolphy.

notes: Stored wiki page matches: 1962 Carmen McRae tribute to Billie Holiday on Columbia; the prose claims are confirmed (including 'died two years previously', which mirrors the wiki text). MB returned no candidates at all, so the tracklist check rests on the wiki's complete 14-track listing: 'Good Morning Heartache' is absent; Lover Man, God Bless the Child, and Them There Eyes match. Separate flag for the repair pass: the item id 'modern-sounds' does not correspond to this album's title - looks like a leftover id from a different record; check the catalog row.

MB embedded confirms Nat King Cole, 1962, Capitol ST-1838, and all 5 keyTracks. No wikipedia evidence (no stored URL; opensearch empty). Unverifiable substantive claims: arranged by Belford Hendricks; LP peaked at number 24 on the Billboard albums chart; title track at number two for two weeks on Easy Listening and number 13 on the Hot 100. No contradictions found.

MB (search, artist similarity 1.0; New Jazz NJ 8270, 1962) and wiki match. Prose mirrors the wiki lead: third album, released 1962 on New Jazz, quintet co-led with Booker Little, Byard/Carter/Haynes returning, recorded the same day Dolphy took part in Ornette Coleman's Free Jazz session, the day after Jazz Abstractions - all confirmed. All 4 keyTracks match ('Mrs. Parker of K.C.' matches MB's 'Mrs. Parker of K.C. (Bird's Mother)').

MB (search, artist similarity 1.0; Blue Note BLP 4112, 1962) and wiki match. Prose mirrors the wiki page: Sonny Clark/Butch Warren/Billy Higgins, recorded August 27 1962, released the same year on Blue Note, the Ira Gitler liner-note quote, Rudy Van Gelder at Englewood Cliffs. All 4 keyTracks match. 'Marked Gordon's comeback' and 'Cheese Cake became definitive' are editorial; Cheese Cake is on the tracklist.

MB (search, artist similarity 1.0; Blue Note BLP 4115, 1962) and wiki match. Prose is the wiki lead verbatim: recorded October 10 1962, BLP 4115/BST 84115, Spaulding/Hancock/Workman/Jarvis, Reid Miles cover design, Francis Wolff photography. All 4 keyTracks match. One discrepancy: wiki shortDescription says '1963 studio album' while MB says first release 1962 (= our year field); sources conflict on release year and ours sides with MB - noted for human review, not flagged as error.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: releaseGroup.firstReleaseDate: "1963-04" (release date also 1963-04, Blue Note BST 84106)

notes: MB match is confident (search, artist similarity 1.0, Blue Note BST 84106) and says first release April 1963, contradicting our year 1962; all 4 keyTracks match its tracklist, so it is clearly the same album. 1962 may be the recording year, but the dataset elsewhere uses release year (e.g. Shirley's Sounds: recorded 1958, year 1961). No usable wikipedia evidence: no stored URL, and opensearch resolved to an unrelated pop-duo page ('Jonnie and Brookie') - discarded. Unverifiable from pack: personnel (Walter Davis Jr., Herbie Lewis, Billy Higgins) and the Ornette Coleman inspiration; the breakthrough/transitional framing is editorial.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: It was recorded on September 17, 1962, and released in February 1963 by United Artists Jazz.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: released in February 1963 by United Artists Jazz
wrongClaims.problem: Contradicts the embedded-MBID MB release date, which the pipeline treats as authoritative for date; note this prose sentence is itself verbatim from the entity-matched wiki page, so the two evidence sources conflict with each other

notes: Right album; the evidence sources split on release date (MB embedded: 1962; wiki: recorded September 17 1962, released February 1963). Ours is internally inconsistent - year field 1962 but prose says 'released in February 1963' - so one of the two is wrong whichever source is right; repair must reconcile (fieldIssues documents year-vs-wiki, wrongClaims documents prose-vs-MB). Everything else checks: Ellington/Mingus/Roach trio, United Artists Jazz, all 5 keyTracks on both tracklists, stored wiki link correct. Also: the artist field lists only Charles Mingus while both sources bill Duke Ellington first (with Mingus and Roach co-credited; MB has 'Charlie Mingus'); the prose attributes the album correctly to Ellington, so this is selective credit of a co-billed artist rather than a contradiction - flag for editorial review.

MB embedded confirms the album (Nancy Wilson / Cannonball Adderley, Capitol ST 1657, 1962-09) and all 5 keyTracks. The stored wikipedia link is wrong: it points to "Somethin' Else (Cannonball Adderley album)", the 1958 Blue Note record with Miles Davis - a different album entirely. Discarded for fact-checking; the site is showing users a wrong link - fix to the Nancy Wilson/Cannonball Adderley album article. With only MB usable, several substantive prose claims are unverifiable: Nat Adderley on cornet and Joe Zawinul on piano, Wilson's 'easy-going third horn' quote, and the claim it reached the pop charts. No contradictions found. Minor: artist field lists only Cannonball Adderley while MB co-credits Nancy Wilson (the title itself names both).

MB embedded (Blue Note BST 84085, 1962-05) and wiki match. Prose is the wiki lead verbatim: released on Blue Note in 1962 as BLP 4085/BST 84085, 2003 CD remaster with two alternate takes, performances by Hubbard, Bernard McKinney, Wayne Shorter, McCoy Tyner, Art Davis and Elvin Jones - all confirmed. All 5 keyTracks match.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: featuring performances recorded and released on the Blue Note label in 1961

notes: MB embedded doc is a 1999 Japanese reissue (Blue Note TOCJ-9143) of our album; its release-group gives first release 1962, agreeing with our year field but contradicting the prose's 'recorded and released... in 1961' - the prose contradicts our own year field too. All 5 keyTracks are on the reissue tracklist. Unverifiable from pack: 'second album', the '1941 Harry Warren song' attribution for 'At Last', and the Etta James R&B/Hot 100 chart-timing claim. No wikipedia evidence (no stored URL; opensearch empty).
